We want the unjust low wages for immigrants to disappear, as they also distort average wages, particularly in sectors such as construction, agriculture, domestic work, and hospitality.
These measures require great courage and political will, as there are numerous vested interests and substantial amounts of money involved in the underground economy.
Precisely in difficult times, it is more important than ever to manage migration flows intelligently—with generosity, but also with responsibility.
The easy thing would be to succumb to the temptation of not attempting to address the underground economy.
We cannot turn a blind eye and leave around eight million irregular immigrants in a state of vulnerability, enduring working conditions approaching slavery.

Mr. President, if we wish for this Directive to be effective, rigorous inspections are necessary, along with economic penalties and even criminal sanctions in the most aggravated cases, in order to have a deterrent effect on business operators.
We will thus succeed in reducing the informal economy and eliminating the migration incentive created by the possibility of working irregularly.
It must be clear that the only option for working in Europe is through legal channels.

Therefore, we wish to continue with the global approach and request the Commission, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Barrot, to present the new "blue cards" for the other categories of workers as soon as possible.

Just as this Parliament has repeatedly called for the European Union to be equipped with the necessary instruments to speak with one voice in the world, the Chamber itself is also aware of the need to remain cohesive in assessing and advancing the major priorities in foreign and security policy.

Our group's commitment, Grupo Sociala, has followed a dual path: on one hand, introducing or reinforcing major issues that are central to us, running across all aspects of foreign policy, such as the fight against climate change, the promotion of

Peace throughout the planet or a commitment to human development. On the other hand, propose elements for readjusting the geographical priorities of fishing relative to how they originally appeared in the text, if at all, or introduce them as new elements if they were absent.

In this way, we have addressed, for example, the need for greater clarification of the relationships between the institutional frameworks and cooperation mechanisms involved in the recently defined Eastern dimension.

We have advocated for greater diversification and greater relevance in our relations with Africa, a continent that we often only remember when particularly virulent wars occur, and sometimes not even then.

Regarding Latin America, we have wished to reflect the current processes of negotiating association agreements—the first of a guiding nature that the European Union will conclude in its entire history.

Regarding the Mediterranean, we have adopted a reductionist approach that speaks only of security, thereby excluding instead the entire rich political, economic, and socio-cultural heritage contained within the Barcelona process.

Regarding the amendments to the Po, our Group has not submitted any, as we believe that doing so would in some way undermine the balance achieved through the compromises, and therefore we will oppose most of the amendments in order not to weaken the agreement reached in the Committee on Foreign Affairs.
Mr. President, Venezuela's referendum has concluded with a victory for the "Yes" vote, gracefully acknowledged by the democratic opposition.

It is no less true that there has been no equality of opportunity in the campaign, and that the ruling party has enjoyed overwhelming support from the entire machinery of the State, while the opposition has suffered continuous harassment and coercion.
Even under those conditions, the country has become practically divided into two halves, and it will be very difficult to build the future based solely on one of them.
The European Union must promote dialogue, inclusion, and consensus among Venezuelan political and social actors for the country's benefit.
It is not through visceral attitudes, dismissals, or insults that we will help Venezuela find its way toward a democratic, pluralistic, and free society.

In this regard, we criticize the Venezuelan Government's decision to expel a Spanish eurodeputy, and even more so the manner in which this expulsion was carried out. But we appeal to this Chamber to avoid

that our representatives make statements during their visits to third countries that violate local legislation in force, and even less so that they insult a head of state, however deserving of criticism he may be.
These attitudes are jeopardizing future missions of the European Parliament to other countries.

Our Parliament should not encourage any kind of confrontation with Venezuela's democratic institutions.
On the contrary, it would be incumbent upon the gentleman to provide explanations to this Chamber regarding an action that affects us all.

Ultimately, what we are doing is simply responding to a Commission report on the implementation of Regulation 1206/2001
The Council Regulation of 28 May 2001 on cooperation between the judicial authorities of the Member States in the field of the taking of evidence in civil or commercial matters.
We are in a sphere that is truly non-communitarian; it is an intergovernmental arena in which, from the Council, the Regulation has sought to encourage cooperation among those judicial bodies.
But by doing so, I believe there is a certain lack of rigor on the part of the European Union.

That is, certain measures have been planned, for example, the issuance of practical guidelines, the use of electronic media, etc. However, I acknowledge that it is quite difficult for the Commission to take action.
I know what the Commission has achieved or intends to achieve, but it seems quite difficult to accomplish given the considerable challenges posed by this form of intergovernmental cooperation.

If we are asking the judicial authorities to cooperate with one another, we are talking about 27 countries, judicial authorities handling 22 different languages, with very different legal systems, and we are trying to achieve results.
The Commission has noted that the results are not good, that there is significant delay in these cooperation mechanisms, and certainly we do not have the resources to do so.
The ideal would be to have material resources.
For example, what could the Commission do to assist judicial bodies, especially those at a lower level with limited technological resources such as a videoconferencing system?
Videoconferencing may be a utopia for many small courts across the entire European Union, but it appears to be the only way to achieve it.
I believe the European Union should rely on existing institutions, specifically the European Judicial Network.
We would need to strengthen this institution so that judges themselves, through their cooperation systems, could develop this type of communication.
On the other hand, any training for judges is essential.
This is something the Commission cannot do on its own either.
However, the Commission can stimulate the development of this type of training courses in community-based education and in the knowledge of the various legal systems.
There is a significant difficulty, namely the language issue—that is, how a Spanish judge can communicate with a Finnish judge when their languages are so different.
One cannot expect judges, in addition to having to handle their own national law, which is already quite complex in itself, to also be required to possess knowledge of this type.
What can we do from a practical standpoint to assist judges in the task of interpretation and translation?

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, I am very pleased to be able to conclude this legislative term with the final approval of the last maritime package.

However, I recall that when I arrived at the European Parliament in 1999, we experienced a major maritime disaster—the sinking of the vessel Erika off the French coast—which gave rise to an initial set of legislative proposals aimed at preventing and addressing such accidents.

We thought we had already overcome the danger, but shortly afterward, the coasts of Galicia were devastated by tar, originating from another accident even more severe than the first, that of the ship.

Since then, in the Transport Committee, we have worked on successive legislative packages, striving to overcome the excessive resistance we encountered from shipowners, oil companies, classification societies, and also from certain Member States that rejected our proposals, considering them overly demanding.

Over these ten years, we have adopted various directives and regulations, some of which we have had to revise again because, as a result of the cuts imposed by the Council, they proved ineffective in their initial formulation.

We have tested the rules for the introduction of double-hull vessels, the European Fund for Major Oil Spills, and refuge ports, and now we are reviewing and approving the liability of maritime passenger transporters, the investigation of

maritime accidents, the Community monitoring and information system for maritime traffic, control by the port State of registry, and finally, the Regulation and Directive concerning ship inspection and classification organizations.
And all of this is good and desirable, and should enter into force and be applied by all Member States as soon as possible.
For this reason, I feel satisfied.
But the legislative term ends, I leave Parliament, and I go away with the regret of not having succeeded in making the implementation of existing control systems mandatory.
They are patented to determine when and how much bilge and fuel tank waste has been illegally discharged into the sea by a specific vessel.
That is, a kind of black boxes, tide gauges that can be inspected by maritime authorities each time a vessel arrives at port.

I believe we owe the marine environment not only better care and stricter procedures to address the vast amount of pollution we generate, but also the recognition that failing to act will impact our food chain and our lives, forcing us to pay a heavy price.
I trust that they will not have to wait another ten years to more effectively and efficiently control illegal maritime fishing activities.
Mr. President, the review of the Roettler Directive is a matter of great importance that concerns us all, particularly the peripheral countries that feel themselves victims of this proposal.
The European Commission has decided to address the external costs of road transport by attributing three additional costs: atmospheric pollution, noise pollution, and congestion.

Although the principles underlying the proposal—namely, that the user pays and the polluter pays—are reasonable, the solution proposed is by no means so, as it will be discriminatory toward a sector that has been suffering for months from the disastrous effects of the economic crisis.
Demonizing road freight transport, or anything else, has turned out to be the end.

This proposal, ladies and gentlemen, is ill-timed and, far from achieving the expected goals of sustainable transport, would be a death sentence for a large number of European small and medium-sized enterprises employing thousands of

people in the European Union who daily contribute to the distribution of goods, ensuring products reach their final consumer, a consumer who will see the prices of their products rise due to the implementation of these fees.
Honorable Members, we need a competitive, sustainable, and environmentally friendly transportation system that does not rely on roads, as currently it is the only means of reaching everywhere.
Modal change remains a dream, far from becoming a reality.
European networks, the highways of the sea, cross-border connections remain, in some cases, projects rather than reality.
Honorable Members, when the titan of the global economy sinks, it is not possible to ask the orchestra to continue playing, least of all for joy.

Mr. President, on July 15, 2008, the European Commission adopted a communication addressed to the Council and the Parliament, recommending the establishment of a strategic partnership between the European Union and Mexico.
In turn, on October 13, the European Council on Foreign Relations recognized Mexico as a strategic partner, pending the Parliament's opinion on the matter.
Let us recall that already at the seventh meeting of the Parliamentary Commission Mita European Union of Mexico, held at the end of October last year, we stated that a
The Strategic Partnership between Mexico and the European Union represents a necessary impetus to strengthen and develop the true potential of our bilateral relations.
We highlight the favorable moment in relations between both parties and the positive outcome of the current comprehensive agreement.

We emphasize the need for our respective executives to strengthen political, economic, and cooperation collaboration, particularly in the face of the new challenges of all kinds arising from the financial and economic crisis that has shaken our continents.

With the great country that is Mexico, we share values and objectives such as the advancement of democratic governance, commitment to gender equity, consolidation of the rule of law, equitable and sustainable development, and respect for human rights.
We advocate strengthening cooperation in combating organized crime, terrorism, and drug trafficking based on the principles of shared responsibility and strict adherence to international law.
Consequently, we will support the proposal for recommendations on the European Union-Mexico Strategic Association, which will be submitted to Parliament this morning.

It has been successful in many developing countries, even in some European ones, both as a mechanism for economic activity and as a tool to improve social inclusion and job creation.

But it had not been transferred to the sector or to the community context. And I believe this presents an opportunity to do so, especially given the economic and financial context in which we currently find ourselves.
In this regard, it is imperative to strengthen the initiatives underway here, but further progress is required.
Funds available for this microcredit support structure must be increased.
Efforts must be made to improve access for individuals and businesses that do not have access to loans, and in this regard, I would like to highlight, Madam President, the example of
the European guarantee for microcredit loans as an instrument that can facilitate such access and has been included in the report.
And I conclude by thinking that, undoubtedly, this will lay the foundations for us to create a harmonious framework within the European context that will drive the microcredit sector.

None of that.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, this question, as Mr. Bokland has explained, is particularly timely in light of the Diplomatic Conference on the Ship Recycling Convention, which will be held in May.
Therefore, I would like to congratulate Mr. Bloán not only for the question but also for the resolution he has prepared.

In the People's Party we support the fight against the improper practices and methods being used to harass vessels, and therefore we back Mr. Brokland's resolution proposal. However, there are certain points that concern us.

The first is paragraph 3, which he himself has highlighted, stating that ships in their entirety must be considered hazardous waste and, as a consequence, must fall within the scope of the Basel Convention.

We have submitted an amendment on this section, as there are differing interpretations both within and outside the European Union, and we believe the Basel Convention must be taken into account and not contradicted, but it should be the future international agreement on ship recycling that defines the specific details of its implementation.
The draft resolution also requests in its point eight a financing mechanism based on mandatory contributions from the maritime transport sector.

We find it rather hasty at this moment to speak of mandatory contributions, given that the International Maritime Organization has not yet decided which system will be adopted for this financing. Moreover, I believe the European Commission also lacks a clear stance on this matter.
Finally, in point 15, the Commission is urged to clearly establish that the State with jurisdiction over waste is the responsible State.
We do not believe it is the Commission's responsibility to decide this matter, which should also be resolved through an international agreement.

The key objective is to ensure coherence between the future Ship Recycling Convention and the Basel Convention on the transboundary movement of hazardous wastes and their disposal, taking into account not only the Basel Convention but also all existing legislation.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, colleagues, first of all, I would like to congratulate the rapporteur with whom we share significant agreement on this program, and to congratulate ourselves as well, because during a period of economic crisis, it is essential to maintain a program of this nature that delivers clear positive social impacts.

concerned about the recovery of the financial system, but we are fully aware that social policies, especially those supporting those who need it most—the poorest in the European Union—must expand.

This food distribution program must continue to be regarded as part of the common agricultural policy. We agree with the Commission on this point, not only because European agriculture has a distinctly social character, but also because it is a functional instrument, because it is more necessary than ever at present, and because it must continue to operate.

From the socialist group, just like the rapporteur, we are opposed to entrusting this program exclusively to the European Union and the member states, as this would constitute discrimination among states according to their wealth, and could result in the program being unimplementable in the countries most in need.

It is understood that now, precisely when solidarity is most needed, efforts are being made to save community funds in the most social aspect of CAP, especially considering that in recent years there have been budgetary surpluses in the agricultural budget.
Full community funding is essential for us.
We need to ensure that this program reaches all countries, and above all, the most vulnerable ones.
Forty-three million potential beneficiaries of this program are calling on us to make a greater effort, and in no case should budgetary spending be cut.
Austerity yes, but not at the expense of the poorest.
And it is precisely to ensure that it reaches the citizen of all Member States that the program must cover the entire journey of the distributed food.

Therefore, on behalf of the Socialist Group, for which I am responsible for the report, we have tabled an amendment to ensure that all expenses related to distribution— including transportation, storage, and administrative costs—are financed by community funds.
We also agree with the rapporteur that food should preferably be community-based and of high quality.

The Arctic region is one of the most fragile areas on our planet.
The consequences of unrestricted exploitation of its natural resources would be catastrophic not only for the immediate environment and indigenous population, but for the world as a whole.

The thawing of extensive regions has brought these risks back into focus, making it necessary to establish a new global regulatory framework to protect the Arctic, similar to the one already in place for the Antarctic, despite the differences that have already been highlighted in the debate.

An international treaty is necessary among all involved parties, undoubtedly including the European Union, to protect its unique environment, ensure the full sustainability of all types of human activities, and multilaterally regulate navigation through the newly accessible maritime routes.
The Arctic Council, during its several years of existence, has served as a model of cooperation for managing common challenges.

At this time of difficulty and uncertainty, we must elevate that spirit and sense of unity to higher levels, in order to prevent the countries of the region or other international actors from becoming embroiled in geostrategic rivalries, forgetting that our shared objective must be the preservation of a great common heritage.

Mr. President, the information you provide me is correct, but the impression we, the European parliamentarians, and ultimately the European citizens, have is that we face a very dangerous gap in community law.
At this moment, as fiscal demands continue to intensify across each of the Member States, mobility is nearly impossible.
And when we asked the Commission, the reason for the pragmatic approach is that the Commission never finds support in the Council.

This is a kind of vicious circle whereby the Commission refers us to the Council and the Council refers us back to the Commission. Yet meanwhile, the reality is that citizens living in this Europe we wish to build do not have the possibility of living in different countries or establishing relationships due to the heavy fiscal burden resulting from the lack of fiscal harmonization.

Madam President, I am glad for once that the Commissioner whom Mr. Tajani has come to replace is not present, because Mr. Tajani has the advantage of having been both a Member of Parliament and a European official.
And as a Member of the European Parliament, I know that you have experienced the frustration we European parliamentarians feel regarding the implementation of community law.

Now, in Parliament we tend to target the Commission. I believe we are asking the Commission to perform an impossible task, because all Community law and the enforcement of Community law are based on indirect application.

That is, the Commission has only a few officials at its headquarters, where some complaints arrive and where there are limited opportunities for action. But at this moment, furthermore, the trend is to restrict budgetary powers, meaning the Commission will no longer have the ability to act.
All Community law. the application of Community law entirely relies on the actions of national authorities, national parliaments, national courts, and national officials.
And here, in this respect, I do not believe we can reasonably expect too much from the Commission.
What we need to do is assist the Commission, and I believe that in Mrs. Frazoni's report, there are certain points that could be helpful in achieving this implementation.
I am referring to those relating to the correlation of directives, the collaboration of national parliaments, and the actions of national courts.
Mr. President, two years ago, during a mission led by my colleague and fellow member of the group present here, María Án, I had the opportunity to visit.
Sierra and being present in this court and being aware of the immense work this court was carrying out not only for Sierra Leone but for all of humanity.
The Special Court of Sierra Leone, as I have already explained here, has undoubtedly set a precedent.

A precedent, because, as the Resolution rightly states, it is the first international tribunal funded by voluntary contributions, the first court established in the country where the alleged crimes took place, and also, as already noted, the first tribunal to try a former prime minister.

For all these reasons, not only because it sets a precedent, but also because it serves as a reference for other tribunals established in its wake, such as those for Rwanda, former Yugoslavia, Cambodia, or Lebanon, we consider it essential that this Resolution be adopted, which we have been working on jointly with the tribunal for several months already.
Two years ago we adopted a resolution to support its funding; two years ago the court was going through a difficult period during which it was running out of
money and did not have sufficient support; here we must also thank the European Commission for providing financial support to this court

Now we are asking, above all, for two things: first, that those sentenced carry out their sentences. What is at stake is no longer merely the functioning of the Tribunal, which will conclude next year, but the legacy this Tribunal will leave behind. And second, obviously, all of this must be accompanied by increased funding.
In short, the Sierra Leone Special Court is an example and a reference for all of us and for all courts that have adjudicated war crimes.
It is an example, a reference, and a lesson that the second poorest country on this planet is teaching us, where, upon entering the courthouse, we saw a motto written: "No justice, no peace."
Precisely for that reason, we have a moral obligation—not only as Europeans but as human beings—that the legacy of this court remain permanent in history

Mr. President, I believe the regulatory proposals submitted to our committee are important and necessary. On the other hand, it was also important and necessary that, from the
The European Parliament insisted on the principles emphasized by the two rapporteurs of the European Parliament, Messrs. Viesca and Deré, namely the principle of community competence.

This is a matter of community competence, in which, for practical reasons, it is advisable to maintain certain competences for the Member States, but, as Mr. Barrot has stated, limited in scope and duration.
The Community competence is not available, and neither the Council, nor the Commission, nor the Parliament has the authority to waive those Community competences.

Having established this, it is an exceptional procedure. I believe the amendments we have discussed and submitted—and which I expect will be approved by Parliament tomorrow—will allow the adoption of this package of measures in first reading.

Madam President, Commissioner, I have signed this resolution and requested to speak today because I was a witness to a forced expulsion that, in essence, was the kidnapping of our colleague Luis Herrrero.

Moreover, I was able to confirm that in Venezuela it is commonplace for political persecution, abuse of power by the Chavista regime, intimidation of opponents, threats, disregard for human dignity, and the misuse of justice for partisan purposes to occur, among other things.
The case of Manuel Chávez is the final drop that has filled the cup and led to this resolution, but there are thousands of cases as egregious as his, cited in the resolution and others that are not.
As is the case with Ligio Cedeño, who was born in the humble Caracas neighborhood of Petare, well known for its danger and also because he voted for a mayor who was not from the Chavista regime.

Eligio studied thanks to the help of others, and specifically with support from Citibank, he managed to establish his own business, Banco de Caracas, and furthermore, lived normally thereafter by helping those in greatest need.
Today, however, he remains imprisoned in a Caracas prison, illegally detained without any charges after being arrested two years ago.
His only crime was being part of the economic oligarchy.
Another case is Nison Moreno, a student leader at the Universidad de los Andes, repeatedly elected to the University Council and president of the Federation of University Centers.
He won the 2013 elections for the current Ministry of the Interior and Justice, and this is his crime.
He is currently charged with homicide in the stage of frustration, violent lascivious acts, and all that despite having been acquitted of specific charges.

Situations such as yours are part of everyday life in this country, where persecuting opponents with the intent of excluding them from political life and discouraging dissent have become routine occurrences.

However, from here it is necessary to send a message of hope to Venezuelan democracy. Despite the difficulties, I am confident that democracy will prevail and that President Chávez will be removed from office through the ballot box.

Madam President, Commissioner, indeed, it is undeniable that progress has been made in recent times; however, despite all the progress achieved, the south of the Union continues to burn, and it burns more intensely each day.
Therefore, the resolution adopted in this Parliament cannot be just another resolution serving merely to highlight our profound concern over the misfortunes of others.
Forest fires occur due to one cause or another, but no one can deny that they are fueled by climate change.

And, regardless of any responsibilities that might exist in certain states or autonomous communities, the fact remains that this Parliament must clearly urge the Commission to act swiftly and to mobilize all available resources, which are by no means insignificant.
Neither the victims, nor the damages suffered, nor the needs for reforestation understand bureaucracy, nor can they comprehend budgetary shortfalls.

Priority must be established, and action must be taken. And acting in this case means adjusting strategies, mobilizing resources, increasing them, and optimally coordinating preventive policies, as required.

In case of, responsibilities for states that lack them. But assuming this is not a problem confined to some southern European states, and that climate change does not affect only certain states, but rather affects all, and is a shared concern.
Therefore, one of the most immediate tasks facing this Parliament must be to ensure that the European Union secures clear commitments at the upcoming Copenhagen Summit.
robust measures to maximize the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, because they are evidently at the root of fires, their causes, and these, in turn, are causes of further climate change.
Therefore, adopting this Resolution is now undoubtedly the challenge facing this Parliament.
But that challenge set by the Commission will be met only if, truly, Mr. Commissioner, you want to prevent the south of the Union, which does exist, from continuing to burn.
Mr. President, thank you very much for granting me the floor. I address the Commissioner Barroso.

Mr. Barroso, I must tell you that I have followed you with great interest and deep respect for a long time, ever since you were in power in a country that is very close to us and holds a special place in our hearts—Portugal.
I must tell you, however, that you now have me absolutely astonished.

I am surprised for two reasons: first, because you have shown disregard for the Group to which I belong, the Group of Non-Inscribed Members, not deigning even a moment of your time to come and present your political proposals to us.
We represent many European citizens, and you have demonstrated contempt for all of our voters.
Second, due to the fragility of their political project and the poverty of their political proposals in the document "Orientaciones para la próxima Comisión."

And the European Union could cease producing those 80 million eggs, those two million tons, and we would merely end up meeting demand through imports from third countries whose animal welfare standards are significantly lower than those of the European Union if we do not act with great intelligence.
This 1999 Directive requiring an increase in the space provided in cages for laying hens obliges us to act with intelligence and a forward-looking, pragmatic vision, because otherwise

We would only succeed in weakening European production and providing additional commercial opportunities to third countries, where safety standards are infinitely worse than the current ones we have in the European Union.

It is a directive that requires enormous efforts from European producers. In Spain, we can estimate that it raises costs to around 600 million euros, but we must not forget that it also has an economic impact extending to the by-products industry and the food industry as a whole.

I therefore request the support of your Lordships for the Popular Group's amendment to the second paragraph, which calls for seeking a solution, at least for responsible farming operations—responsible industrial operators—who are currently undergoing transformation and will be nearly completed by January 2012.

We must support their transformation of these facilities and allow them sufficient time to complete the process, thus avoiding irreparable harm to farms and preventing an abrupt shortage in the community market, which would consequently drive up prices for consumers.

It is about respecting the directive to provide an opportunity to the egg and egg by-product industry, while also upholding the rights related to animal welfare and consumers' right to a reasonable price.

Accounting audits today are hindered by the concentration and lack of independence among auditors, and they fail to generate the much-desired confidence needed to revive our economy.
It would not be unreasonable to consider eliminating the legal requirement for audit reviews.
But why not?

If we wish to regain the confidence of markets and maintain that confidence within markets, we must carry out a thorough reform of audits so that they provide genuine added value and not merely a legal burden.
Major auditing firms use audit services as a calling card to attract future clients.
Once familiar with the company, they offer more expensive complementary services that often offset the low prices of the strict audit.
We have a problem of price distortion in the sector.

These complementary services already account for nearly 50 percent of revenue, so major auditors do not want to lose audit services—not because of the audits themselves, but to maintain and expand the complementary services.
The second major obstacle to free competition in the sector is the oligopoly of the four large companies that dominate European markets by more than 90 percent.
As such an oligopoly, they control prices and establish barriers to entry for new competitors, leveraging intangibles such as international brand image.

It is true that this brand image may be based on dietary advantages, but it does not always imply greater efficiency due to the diversity of legislation in the accounting states, which may be treated differently in each Member State.
I share the objectives of the Green Paper, namely the independence and positioning of the sector, as well as the means to be used—rotation, joint audits.

Today women can work without having to ask permission from anyone, but in many cases they still earn less than men for similar work, which is unacceptable.
One must also remember the women in rural areas whose work goes unrecognized.
I believe another uncomfortable reality, yet one that must continue to be denounced, is violence against women.

This year alone, an excessive number of women have been murdered, not counting the serious threats made by those who believe they have the right to inflict physical or psychological violence upon them.
And when it comes to saying "down" and saying it loud and clear.
From this Parliament, I reaffirm my commitment to the European Union’s Victims’ Rights Directive and to the Victims’ Directive presented by the Commission, which we hope to vote on next June.
We must also say—and I would like to emphasize—that motherhood continues to be an obstacle to women's advancement, and I believe this trend should be changed.
I want to send a message of hope, and with many women, with the growing support of men, we must advance along the path of equality, opportunities, rights, and also of responsibilities.
We have come a long way, but there is much still to be done.
I must admit that I do not absolutely and deeply believe in quotas, but I must tell you that something needs to be done, and I agree.

Mr. President, at a time of economic crisis and budget cuts affecting public health—such as in the community where I live, where research centers like the Prince Felipe, specialized in diabetes, are left without funding—this resolution comes at a more appropriate moment than ever.

We need a common European strategy against diabetes, and we need a recommendation from the Council stating that Member States must increase research, including research involving stem cells, must coordinate their findings to optimize them, and must establish programs promoting healthy eating and lifestyles.

In short, we must place people's health above all else, and in this case, investigate, coordinate, and implement national programs coordinated by the Commission and demanding results. Addressing diabetes is about ensuring the quality of life of Europeans and securing their very lives.

If we allocate more funding to this, some people will take a little longer to receive payments, but undoubtedly those currently facing difficulties will enjoy a better quality of life, and we, as Member States, as the Council, and as the Commission, will be fulfilling one of our obligations—ensuring the health of our citizens.

the European social model in which we take so much pride is not based solely on our pension, education, or healthcare systems, but also on a particular way of understanding the balance between employer and employee within companies.

The labor reform approved by the Spanish government without dialogue or consensus breaks with that social model and moves us away from the objectives of the 2020 Strategy, both in terms of job creation and in terms of job quality.
A labor reform by itself does not create jobs; economic growth does.
What labor law does do is define under what conditions workers enter companies, under what conditions they remain in them, and under what conditions they leave them.

And with this reform, where 80% of workers will be left unprotected because they will not be subject to collective agreements, making them vulnerable to dismissal due to a decline in company revenues or

They may unilaterally modify their schedules; what is clear, however, is that workers will enter the labor market with greater precarity, remain in it with increased fear, and leave it with reduced compensation.

That is why this reform, which undermines the European social model and seeks competitiveness of the Spanish economy solely in relation to workers' conditions, deserves a general strike such as the one that must be called tomorrow in Spain in defense of this social model.

An unbearable figure for anyone who stops to study the terrible consequences of these practices, which are concealed behind the guise of psychological reasons, religious health, all of them false.

The truth is that the ablative procedure performed predominantly on girls from infancy and even as young as one year old can result in death due to hemorrhagic collapse, and in the best-case scenario, cause severe psychological sequelae due to trauma.
From Europe, we must fight with all our strength to eradicate this violation of human rights, which undermines the right to equal opportunities and endangers health.

Mr. President, the rapporteur's work is very good, but I would like to take this opportunity to state here, within this framework, that our prose is highly confusing, as anyone who approaches European legislation can readily observe.
We forget what Puffendorf already taught, that general laws should be few and their meaning must be properly understood.
For them, those who issue it, must use as much clarity as possible.
And Montesquieu consistently emphasized that legal language must be precise and concise.
That is, we must avoid esoteric jargon, filled with neologisms and unjustified linguistic borrowings, because it creates a malignant web where traps thrive and arbitrariness takes root.
We deal with very complex issues in this Parliament, but it is our duty to convey them to citizens in a comprehensible and accessible manner.
It is common for even legal professionals—judges and lawyers alike—to become frustrated when confronted with our texts.
Good prose, dear colleagues, is the legislator's courtesy and a way of demonstrating respect for the citizen.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, there exists an old principle that has moved from the legal sphere into the political realm: pacta sunt servanda, meaning agreements are made to be kept.
By this I mean the significant measures adopted by the European Council in June to address the crisis affecting us.

Since the adoption of these agreements, market pressures eased, and they welcomed decisions that could break the vicious cycle between banks and sovereign debt, as well as plans to advance toward a genuine economic and monetary union and the commitment to defend the euro in an irreversible manner.
The European Council held last week, ladies and gentlemen, contains undeniable positive aspects. The conclusions regarding progress toward a Banking Union are satisfactory.

It is also positive that an agreement has been reached regarding the establishment of the banking supervisor. Finally, it is appropriate that the implementation be entrusted to the ministers of economy of the region.
But they will allow him to make a critical assessment of the biased interpretations that have been unilaterally made of it.

Your Honors, it is unacceptable to make particular interpretations that may distort the mandate granted to the Ministers of Economy, nor do we approve of matters being presented as agreements when they were not even discussed.
It is urgent to move toward a single supervisory mechanism, which must cover all financial institutions.
A common deposit guarantee scheme and a crisis resolution mechanism are also needed.
The survival of the euro is linked to the urgent creation of an effective banking union.
Madam President, ladies and gentlemen, Commissioner, the advantages of the internal market are unquestionable: increased competition, greater availability of products, and.
services, creation of new jobs, more affordable prices for consumers, and greater protection—still insufficient, and we cannot afford complacency.
However, cultural, linguistic, protectionist, and bureaucratic barriers prevent citizens and businesses from fully benefiting from the single market.
Half a year ago, we celebrated the launch of the twelve priorities aimed at stimulating growth and strengthening citizens' confidence in the internal market.
Unfortunately, an agreement has not yet been reached on most of the proposals, as the Commission itself acknowledged in last week's communication.

Mr. President, Commissioner, the urgency of the crisis compels us to be ambitious, to go much further, and to achieve agreements as soon as possible to boost growth, employment, and confidence among citizens and businesses in the single market.
Mr. President, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur and the other colleagues who have worked on this reform very adequately.

The financial crisis has intensified due to the existence of complex and obscure securities linked to derivatives and off-exchange transactions, all aimed at achieving high returns in a short period of time.
It is necessary to bring financial operations into a transparent market to ensure proper price formation and competitiveness of tradable products.
But above all, it is essential that the information provided be understandable to investors, who must have full awareness in order to freely consent to the risks they undertake.
Transparency is of no use as a brilliant flash of light if the confusion surrounding the design of tradable products blinds citizens' understanding.

We too often hear accusations leveled against European institutions regarding their limited democratic quality.
It is a statement made casually; this is not the occasion to enter into this debate.

Yes, to emphasize that this Parliament possesses the same legitimacy as any national parliament, and it is even likely that, with regard to some, it operates under more transparent rules and additionally grants its members greater freedom.

Mr. President, 5.3% of the active population works in the automotive sector.
The figures speak for themselves.
The decline in sales in the European market, combined with corporate strategy, leads, in general terms, to the outsourcing and restructuring of companies.
There is a capacity overload crisis currently affecting ten assembly plants and potentially up to 10,000 workers.

Unfortunately, there are few cases of restructuring that adopt a long-term perspective. Generally, a short-term outlook prevails, in which the main objective is gaining competitiveness through labor adjustments.

I must point out that on the very same day that Ford communicated the shutdown of the Gen plant, the Valencia Ford company was informed that its production would be moved to Germany, in the same corporate announcement.

Therefore, for this reason, we believe it is a general issue for Europe. We believe it is essential to establish a legal framework at the European level that anticipates changes and emphasizes the importance of social dialogue.
Social dialogue is fundamental.
Sir, today we are discussing four reports that represent a revitalization of our common policy on security and defense.
It is a clear indication of the conviction that our security and defense have a distinct European dimension, in contrast to the minority and hygrophilic factions that also prevail in this Chamber.
The report by Mr. Danjián, to whom I wish to express my gratitude for his spirit of consensus-building throughout its preparation, contains significant contributions from the Social Group.
among others, references to effective multilateralism, the responsibility to protect principle, or the concept of human security.
And we would like to see these aspects included in the possible review of the European Defence Strategy.
We have also pointed out that a strong common security and defense policy is of interest to our transatlantic alliance as well.
I must highlight another challenge that is acquiring significant proportions for our global security.
It is the one on cybersecurity and cyberdefense, reports that we have also discussed today, and whose recommendations we hope will be included in the new European Cybersecurity Strategy.
In a context of severe economic and financial crisis, in which member states are being forced to cut their defense budgets, it is precisely when European projects are most necessary.
That is why it is more important than ever to strengthen the role of the European Defence Agency, which is currently facing significant criticism.
I conclude by stating that we can relaunch European security and defense, and do so with President Vor Rapuy's project for the December 2013 Council.

Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, once again, the defense of the general interests championed by the Commission and the Parliament confronts national self-interests that prevail in the Council.
Given the prior acceptance of the legitimacy of both positions, reaching a balance is inexcusable.
The relevance of the upcoming financial framework is unquestionable.
The Spanish delegation of the PPE, and I on its behalf, reaffirm that, in our judgment, the Presidency's proposal is unacceptable.
A cut of 75 billion euros from the already inevitably meager European Commission proposal proves sterile and useless.
And not only that—this new direct cut to agricultural and cohesion policies exceeds the red line that Spain had established from the outset of the negotiations.
Honorable Members, it is no longer the time to debate an alleged dichotomy between authority and growth.
Some of us are making proven efforts and sacrifices to correct the deficit.
But Spain, like many other countries, needs to grow, increase economic activity, and create jobs.
And with this budget, one achieves nothing. On the contrary, it constitutes a missile aimed at the waterline.
Honorable Members, it is not possible to achieve more Europe with fewer resources, and especially not if what exists is distributed unjustly.

If that is the case, Spain will oppose a financial framework for the European Union that amounts to a straightforward elimination of growth opportunities and, above all, any remaining trace of solidarity. And this is precisely the direction the European Council is taking.
Fortunately, this is not the path indicated by the Commission, to which we must express our gratitude for its commitment to charting a different route.

And at this point, in the need for unity, in the need for shared efforts, I wish to thank Mr. Barroso for his firm statement, delivered the other day in Cádiz, interpreting the Treaties in a way that firmly rejects any excessive interpretation within the Union.

He made it clear, unequivocally, as guardian of the Treaties, that if a part of a Member State separates as an independent state, it becomes regarded as a third country, and thus the provisions and effects of the Treaty itself will not apply to it in any way.

I hope, Mr. Vice President, in the absence of the Mr. President, that this may be taken up and that this safe and consistent doctrine of the Commission may continue to remain alive for the protection of all.
